questions.stem: A client one hour after delivery has heavy bleeding and a boggy uterus. Place the nursing actions in the order the nurse should take them.
questions.scenario: The pad is saturated and the client reports dizziness while holding the newborn.

options.text: Massage the fundus
Call for help and bring hemorrhage support to the bedside
Assess bladder fullness and assist with emptying if needed
Prepare uterotonic medication after initial bedside measures

questions.rationale: Heavy postpartum bleeding with a boggy uterus is managed as a hemorrhage problem first. The nurse brings help, massages the fundus, corrects bladder-related atony contributors, and prepares uterotonic support without waiting for routine reassessment.
questions.takeaway: Boggy uterus means act on atony now, not after more observation.
questions.speedCue: On exam day, boggy plus bleeding should push uterine atony to the top immediately.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with sodium 118 mEq/L becomes confused and then has a seizure. Place the actions in the order the nurse should take them.
questions.scenario: Emergency supplies are available and the provider is on the way.

options.text: Prepare to administer hypertonic saline as prescribed
Protect the airway and initiate seizure precautions
Notify the provider or rapid response team with the acute change
Reassess neurologic status after initial stabilization

questions.rationale: In symptomatic hyponatremia, the nurse starts with seizure safety and airway protection, escalates immediately, prepares for controlled hypertonic saline correction, and then reassesses neurologic response. The reasoning is faster when the learner links low sodium with acute neurologic collapse.
questions.takeaway: When low sodium causes seizure, think protect, escalate, correct, then reassess.
questions.speedCue: The key clue is not just a sodium number; it is low sodium plus acute neurologic change.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with acute leukemia is 24 hours after chemotherapy and now has weakness, nausea, peaked T waves, and decreasing urine output. Place the actions in the order the nurse should anticipate.
questions.scenario: Laboratory results are pending repeat confirmation while the client remains on telemetry.

options.text: Notify the provider and treat this as an oncologic emergency
Prepare for rapid management of life-threatening electrolyte instability
Support aggressive hydration and renal protection as ordered
Continue close rhythm and urine-output reassessment

questions.rationale: Tumor lysis syndrome should be answered as an electrolyte-and-kidney emergency after chemotherapy. Rapid recognition, urgent treatment of unstable electrolytes, hydration support, and ongoing reassessment are the core sequence.
questions.takeaway: After chemotherapy, weakness plus peaked T waves plus falling urine output is a tumor lysis emergency pattern.
questions.speedCue: The speed clue is post-chemo electrolyte collapse with telemetry danger, not routine nausea.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: For each client finding, click to specify whether the finding is more consistent with neuroleptic malignant syndrome or less consistent with neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
questions.scenario: The client recently started a high-potency antipsychotic and now has fever and confusion.

options.text: More consistent with NMS
Less consistent with NMS

questions.rationale: NMS is easier to recognize when the learner locks onto severe rigidity and autonomic instability after dopamine-blocking medication exposure. Hyperreflexia and diarrhea push the pattern away from NMS and more toward serotonin syndrome.

distractorRationales.a: "More consistent with NMS" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Hyperreflexia and diarrhea push the pattern away from NMS and more toward serotonin syndrome" points to medication toxicity. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Less consistent with NMS" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before medication-safety intervention.

questions.takeaway: Rigidity after antipsychotic exposure should move NMS higher than serotonin syndrome.
questions.speedCue: When a high-fever medication question includes rigidity, slow down and separate rigidity from hyperreflexia.

visualRationale.title: neuroleptic_malignant_syndrome
visualRationale.caption: Rigidity after antipsychotic exposure should move NMS higher than serotonin syndrome.
visualRationale.conclusion: Rigidity after antipsychotic exposure should move NMS higher than serotonin syndrome.

questions.stem: For each newborn finding, click to specify whether the finding supports hypoglycemia concern or is less specific for hypoglycemia.
questions.scenario: The newborn was delivered by a client with gestational diabetes and has not fed well since birth.

options.text: Supports hypoglycemia concern
Less specific for hypoglycemia

questions.rationale: This item trains quick clue sorting. Jitteriness and weak feeding in a high-risk newborn support hypoglycemia concern; the other findings are less specific and should not distract the nurse from checking and treating glucose promptly.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses unstable glucose physiology; "Supports hypoglycemia concern" moves care toward routine care before glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization.
distractorRationales.b: "Less specific for hypoglycemia" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Jitteriness and weak feeding in a high-risk newborn support hypoglycemia concern" points to unstable glucose physiology. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Newborn jitteriness paired with poor feeding is a check-glucose-now clue set.
questions.speedCue: In a newborn of a diabetic parent, jitteriness is not a soft clue; it should immediately raise glucose concern.

visualRationale.title: newborn_hypoglycemia_jitteriness
visualRationale.caption: Newborn jitteriness paired with poor feeding is a check-glucose-now clue set.
visualRationale.conclusion: Newborn jitteriness paired with poor feeding is a check-glucose-now clue set.

questions.stem: For each finding, click to specify whether the finding is more consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis or less consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis.
questions.scenario: The client reports recurrent episodes of severe headache and pounding palpitations.

options.text: More consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis
Less consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis

questions.rationale: Pheochromocytoma crisis is solved faster when the learner bundles headache, diaphoresis, palpitations, and severe hypertension as a catecholamine surge pattern. Chronic, non-episodic findings push the answer away from this diagnosis.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "More consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before bedside safety action.
distractorRationales.b: "Less consistent with pheochromocytoma crisis" misses the bedside risk. The cue "The client reports recurrent episodes of severe headache and pounding palpitations" points to clinical safety risk. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Headache, diaphoresis, palpitations, and severe episodic hypertension belong together as a pheochromocytoma pattern.
questions.speedCue: The speed clue is episodic sympathetic surge, not slow chronic symptom drift.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: For each maternal finding, click to specify whether the finding is more consistent with placental abruption or less consistent with placental abruption.
questions.scenario: The client is 35 weeks pregnant and arrives after reporting severe abdominal pain.

options.text: More consistent with placental abruption
Less consistent with placental abruption

questions.rationale: Placental abruption becomes easier to answer when the learner locks onto pain, uterine tone, and fetal compromise clues. Painless bright-red bleeding points away from abruption and more toward placenta previa.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury; "More consistent with placental abruption" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before bleeding-risk intervention.
distractorRationales.b: "Less consistent with placental abruption" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "Painless bright-red bleeding points away from abruption and more toward placenta previa" raises concern for bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so bleeding-risk intervention is needed before diagnostic reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Abruption is the painful, rigid, often dark-bleeding pattern.
questions.speedCue: Pain and uterine rigidity should move abruption to the top of the differential before you even finish the stem.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains this infant's pattern?
questions.scenario: An 11-month-old infant has repeated episodes of sudden crying, draws the knees to the chest, then becomes quiet and pale between episodes.
questions.additionalInfo: The parent reports one diaper with dark red mucus and says the infant vomited twice this morning.

options.text: Intussusception requiring urgent evaluation for bowel telescoping and perfusion risk
Simple viral gastroenteritis with expected fussiness
Normal teething behavior with transient abdominal discomfort
Milk intolerance without bowel obstruction concern

questions.rationale: Intermittent pain, knee-to-chest posture, vomiting, and currant-jelly stool are a classic intussusception pattern. The nurse should group those clues as bowel compromise, not as simple gastroenteritis.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Simple viral gastroenteritis with expected fussiness" moves care toward reassurance before circulation and perfusion support.
distractorRationales.c: "Normal teething behavior with transient abdominal discomfort" misses the bedside risk. The cue "An 11-month-old infant has repeated episodes of sudden crying, draws the knees to the chest, then becomes quiet" points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "Intermittent pain, knee-to-chest posture, vomiting" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Alternating distress and quiet periods with bloody mucus stool should move bowel telescoping high on the list.
questions.speedCue: The fastest clue cluster is episodic pain plus knees to chest plus currant-jelly stool.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which nursing concern is most important in this third-trimester client?
questions.scenario: A client at 32 weeks' gestation arrives with painless bright-red vaginal bleeding after getting out of bed.
questions.additionalInfo: The abdomen is soft, the fetal heart rate is reassuring, and the client says there has been no trauma and no severe pain.

options.text: Placenta previa pattern with a need to avoid vaginal examination and monitor maternal-fetal status
Placental abruption with concealed hemorrhage as the most likely explanation
Normal cervical change in late pregnancy
Benign spotting that can be reassessed at the next office visit

questions.rationale: Painless bright-red third-trimester bleeding with a soft uterus fits placenta previa. The nurse should prioritize maternal-fetal monitoring and avoid actions that can worsen bleeding.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave bleeding or anticoagulation injury untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Placenta previa pattern with a need to avoid vaginal examination and monitor maternal-fetal status" fits the safer response to the cue "pain and a firm tender uterus push you away from it".
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave bleeding or anticoagulation injury untreated because it normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. "Placenta previa pattern with a need to avoid vaginal examination and monitor maternal-fetal status" fits the safer response to the cue "pain and a firm tender uterus push you away from it".
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury; "Benign spotting that can be reassessed at the next office visit" moves care toward routine observation before bleeding-risk intervention.

questions.takeaway: Painless bleeding points you toward previa; pain and a firm tender uterus push you away from it.
questions.speedCue: Solve this one by pairing the bleeding color with the absence of pain or uterine rigidity.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which conclusion best matches this infant's feeding pattern?
questions.scenario: A 6-week-old infant has progressive projectile vomiting after nearly every feeding and seems hungry again right away.
questions.additionalInfo: The infant has fewer wet diapers, a visible wave across the abdomen during feeds, and weight gain has stalled.

options.text: Pyloric stenosis with dehydration risk and gastric outlet obstruction
Normal reflux that only needs thicker formula
Acute gastroenteritis with self-limited vomiting
Food allergy with no obstructive pattern

questions.rationale: Progressive projectile vomiting, hunger after emesis, visible peristaltic waves, and poor weight gain fit pyloric stenosis. The nurse should connect the obstructive pattern early and watch hydration closely.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave pediatric or newborn instability untreated because it normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. "Pyloric stenosis with dehydration risk and gastric outlet obstruction" fits the safer response to the cue "A 6-week-old infant has progressive projectile vomiting after nearly every feeding and seems hungry again right away".
distractorRationales.c: "Acute gastroenteritis with self-limited vomiting" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Projectile vomiting that is followed by hunger points toward obstruction, not simple spit-up" points to pediatric or newborn instability. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "A 6-week-old infant has progressive projectile vomiting after nearly every feeding and seems hungry again right away" makes pediatric or newborn instability the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Projectile vomiting that is followed by hunger points toward obstruction, not simple spit-up.
questions.speedCue: The combination of projectile vomiting and wanting to feed again is the clue that speeds you to pyloric stenosis.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client in labor has a sudden fetal heart rate drop after rupture of membranes and the nurse sees a loop of umbilical cord. Which immediate actions should the nurse take?
questions.scenario: The fetal heart rate remains in the 70s while help is being called.

options.text: Use a gloved hand to lift the presenting part off the cord
Position the client to relieve pressure on the cord
Attempt to push the visible cord back into the vagina
Delay intervention until the provider is at the bedside
Call for immediate obstetric assistance
Start coached pushing with the next contraction

questions.rationale: Cord prolapse is a compression emergency. The nurse's first moves are to relieve the pressure, position the client to reduce compression, and summon immediate obstetric help. Pushing the cord back or delaying action increases fetal hypoxia risk.

distractorRationales.c: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Use a gloved hand to lift the presenting part off the cord plus Position the client" fits the safer response to the cue "A client in labor has a sudden fetal heart rate drop after rupture of membranes and the nurse".
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Use a gloved hand to lift the presenting part off the cord plus Position the client" fits the safer response to the cue "A client in labor has a sudden fetal heart rate drop after rupture of membranes and the nurse".
distractorRationales.f: "Start coached pushing with the next contraction" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "Cord prolapse is a compression emergency" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before diagnostic reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Cord prolapse means relieve compression now, not when the provider arrives.
questions.speedCue: If you can see the cord and the heart rate crashes, think pressure-off-the-cord immediately.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with a positive pregnancy test reports sudden unilateral pelvic pain, dizziness, and left shoulder pain. Which findings support the nurse's concern for a ruptured ectopic pregnancy?
questions.scenario: The client looks pale and anxious in triage. Blood pressure is 86/48 mm Hg and heart rate is 128/min.

options.text: Hypotension
Shoulder pain
Rigid abdomen
Light pink spotting only
Tachycardia
Improving pelvic discomfort after rest

questions.rationale: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy should be recognized quickly from the clue cluster of unilateral pain, hypotension, tachycardia, and referred shoulder pain from intraperitoneal bleeding. The nurse should connect these findings to hemorrhagic instability instead of treating them as routine first-trimester discomfort.

distractorRationales.c: "Rigid abdomen" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Ruptured ectopic pregnancy should be recognized quickly from the clue cluster of unilateral pain, hypotension, tachycardia" points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "Ruptured ectopic pregnancy should be recognized quickly from the clue cluster of unilateral pain, hypotension, tachycardia" suggests bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so "Light pink spotting only" would not correct the active problem. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.f: "Improving pelvic discomfort after rest" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Ruptured ectopic pregnancy should be recognized quickly from the clue cluster of unilateral pain, hypotension, tachycardia" raises concern for bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so bleeding-risk intervention is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: When pregnancy, unilateral pain, shoulder pain, and shock signs appear together, think ruptured ectopic fast.
questions.speedCue: Pregnancy plus unilateral pain plus shoulder pain plus hypotension is a hemorrhage pattern, not a routine discomfort pattern.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.
